deck: Returning to a mango tree in Tanzania, where in 2008 Dimagi co-designed CommCare alongside the community health workers who were its earliest testers. A conversation about privacy, trust, and the human relationships behind the technology.
seo_description: Revisiting CommCare's origins in 2008 Tanzania, where community health workers co-designed the app under a mango tree and shaped features still used today.
og_description: How community health workers in Tanzania co-designed CommCare under a mango tree in 2008, and why it still matters.
about: This episode revisits the origins of CommCare in Tanzania, returning to the community health workers who were among its very earliest testers. Amie Vaccaro is joined by Brian DeRenzi, Dimagi's global director of research, and Gayo, who worked with Dimagi in the early days of CommCare in 2008, recruiting and training community health workers to use the application on their phones as they delivered home based care.

Gayo recently went back to the Kiba area in Tanzania and held five conversations with some of those first community health workers, recording and transcribing their reflections on what changed when they moved from paper to a small mobile phone. They discuss the heavy stigma around HIV at the time, how a discreet device protected client privacy where bulky paper registers had drawn attention, and how features like password protection, referral tracking, a demo practice mode, and a supervisor tool emerged directly from the workers' own suggestions.

Running through the conversation is the philosophy Dimagi calls design under the mango tree: sitting with the people you aim to serve, handing them a pen and paper, and letting them shape the tool. As Brian reflects, the technology ultimately boils down to relationships, the trust health workers build with their communities and the trust Gayo built with the health workers themselves.

pull_quote.text: These community health workers, they're just people who are not well educated probably, but if you give them a chance, give them a piece of paper and say, how would you like the application to be? They'll just draw down what they think the application should be.
pull_quote.cite: Gayo, on designing under the mango tree

bullets: Design under the mango tree: how Dimagi sat with health workers in Kiba, Tanzania in 2008 to co-design CommCare from their paper forms
Privacy by design: why a discreet phone reduced HIV stigma where bulky paper registers had exposed clients, and how password protection was born
Features the workers asked for: referral tracking, follow-up reminders, a demo practice mode, and a separate supervisor tool all emerged from their suggestions
The genesis of case management: how a request to keep a list of patients led to one of CommCare's most important capabilities
Teaching the belly button and delete: the trainings that built digital literacy among workers who had never used a phone
It boils down to relationships: Brian DeRenzi on why the technology matters only because of the human trust behind it
A vision for interoperability: Gayo on fully digitizing health services from community to facility to referral hospital
